column2:
The Communication capability supports:
1. External communication: enable European citizens to understand what the European Parliament is, what it does and what it stands for, by employing a variety of channels and tools.
The ultimate goal of all external communication efforts is to ensure that people understand the importance of the European Parliament well enough to engage in the European democratic process.
2. Internal communication: enable the EP administration to communicate effectively, creating transparency and awareness over their internal offerings and state of play.
column4: The Event Management Capability  is comprised of the periodic planning, promotion, running and evaluation of IT focused events. For example CSE/CUSTEX: Organise IT events such as DIGITEC, Digital accessibility events, working groups, task forces.
column6: The Event Organisation Capability is the ability to organise IT events such as DIGITEC, Digital accessibility events, working groups, task forces. Adding a comment for testing

column4: The Innovation Promotion/Engagement capability is the ability to promote an innovation mindset across the EP. Central to this is the EP Innovate platform whereby users can learn more about innovation and actively contribute to the promotion of an innovation mindset through the platform's content sharing capabilities. Innovation Space Managers can publish articles and run discussions in their spaces, which offers a way to help users stay inspired, engaged and informed about innovation good practices. The Technological Innovation Space, for example, regularly publishes Tech News updates and runs online Discussions on innovation topics, in addition to gathering innovation ideas in the Ideation corner. Innovation Space Managers are supported by the INNOVIT team through coaching, while the Innovators Playbook offers a quick getting started guide to anyone interested in becoming a promoter of innovation at the EP.
column6: The INNOVIT Events & Seminars Management capability is the ability to conceive and deliver events and seminars to various EP internal stakeholders in the context of innovation to create awareness and interest in this domain and stimulate an innovation mindset. For example, DG ITEC Innovation Week enables EP personnel to participate in expert talks, live interactive experiences with emerging technologies and demonstrations from researchers, entrepreneurs and the public sector.

The Innovation Training capability is the ability to provide training and learning in the area of innovation. The training can be general for a wider audience or more tailored to specific clients' needs. 
 
For example, Innovation Space Managers on the EP INNOVATE platform are supported by the INNOVIT team through coaching, while the Innovators Playbook offers a quick getting started guide to anyone interested in becoming a promoter of innovation at the EP.

column5: EP Data Science Lab Content & Community Management
column6: The EP Data Science Lab Content and Community Management capability is the ability to run and evolve the EP Data Science Portal where the data science community can meet to share best practices, knowledge and expertise on AI and data analytics. The platform is part of DG ITEC’s effort to support data-driven decision-making both at the political and at the administrative level in the EP. It will facilitate innovation by fostering the usage of data science methodologies. Ultimately, this will lead to increased data literacy and a thriving data culture. Through actively engaging practitioners across the EP, the aim is to encourage and support innovative initiatives in the fields of data science, data analytics and AI, view of paving the way towards the productive implementation of innovative data-driven technologies such as Artificial Intelligence (AI), Machine Learning and Big Data analytics at the European Parliament.

column4:
Multi-channel Publishing is the capability to make information/content available to people in different formats (digital and print) and visual representations across different mediums and communication channels. It concerns the design, production and distribution of publishing products to internal clients (MEPS and their staff, political groups, the EP Administration and on occasion to other EU Institutions - it does not cover any type of direct communications to citizens or other external stakeholders). 
 
Examples of print publishing products include leaflets, brochures, EP diaries, EP calendar, roll-up banners and examples of digital publishing products include interactive PDFs, eNewsletters, infographics, eBanners, ePosters and animations.
column6:
The capability to manage the client requests for all of ITEC's publishing products and services offered to EP clients.  
 
The Publishing Service Desk (PSD) is the single point of client contact for a wide range of professional publishing products and services. PSD client managers handle orders from placement to the delivery of the final product, ensuring the highest quality standards in the shortest delay for the EP client. Client managers follow each job through from inception to completion. EP clients can also view and order digital and traditional print products online via the Publishing & Documents Catalogue portal.

column6:
Produce the institution's printed matter and publications (color printing for the work of Deputies, political groups and the Administration). 
Produce the institution's working documents (pure legislative printing). 
Printing in B&W and in colour. Legislative work is in B&W printing. The process of printing has various steps: 
• Coordination phase 
• Printer phase 
• Finishing phase 
• Expedition 
Digital printing is the process of printing digital-based images directly onto a variety of media substrates. There is no need for a printing plate, unlike with offset printing.

column6: Supporting a paperless Parliament, the digital publishing capability (sometimes called electronic publishing), concerns the ability to create digital (non-print) publication products that can be presented on computer screens and other types of digital screens and digital devices that are available across the EP. In this way, information becomes discoverable, accessible and presentable in a more dynamic and economic way, thus enriching the communication and engaging the target audience in a more attractive way. Examples of digital publication products include: ePosters, eBanners, animations, virtual backgrounds, social media templates and infographics.

The Print Publishing capability is the ability to provide graphic design and prepare publications and products for printing. In the context of the EP, printed products include leaflets, brochures, posters, roll-up banners and stationary items such as business cards, letter head etc. Publications include EP diaries, EP calendar, Financial Regulations and Fact sheets on the European Union. There are also specific print products related to Protocol such as Protocol Table Plans. 
 
EP clients can order print products online via the Publishing & Documents Catalogue portal or by contacting the Publishing Service Desk.

Secure printing and secure scanning capability contains the following: 
• There are associated services regarding the secure printing (from any device not only from workstations or hybrids but also from private devices via email). 
• Secure scan: there are different options (scan to my own folder - which is only accessible for the user, scan2mail with the limits of email - size up to 15 MBs, scan2Geda). Depending also on the user profile there are different functionalities and services. All these are fully compliant with the Data Protection Regulation.

1. External communication: enable European citizens to understand what the European Parliament is, what it does and what it stands for, by employing a variety of channels and tools.
The ultimate goal of all external communication efforts is to ensure that people understand the importance of the European Parliament well enough to engage in the European democratic process.
2. Internal communication: enable the EP administration to communicate effectively, creating transparency and awareness over their internal offerings and state of play.
column4:
The Relationship Management (REM) capability aims to ensure that liaison and long-term 
interaction between the IT function and other business units foster business awareness, 
mutually align interests, and help minimize issues of conflict.
column6:

this feedback loop.

column6:
Asset management sub-area deals with the major asset classes of the EP: 
 * Tangible assets contain various subclasses, including current assets and fixed assets. Current assets include inventory, while fixed assets include such items as buildings and equipment.
 * Intangible assets are nonphysical resources and rights that have a value to the firm because they give the firm some kind of advantage in the market place. Examples of intangible assets are goodwill, copyrights, trademarks, patents and computer programs and financial assets, including such items as accounts receivable, bonds and stocks.

A recovery order, and a debit note for the debtor, must be drawn up by the authorising officer responsible for any amount receivable that is identified as being certain, of a fixed amount and due. The recovery order is the operation by which the authorising officer responsible instructs the accounting officer to recover the amount established as receivable.
The accounting officer is responsible for recovering the amount receivable. He/she exercises due diligence to ensure that the Union receives its revenue and shall ensure that its rights are safeguarded.

column4:
The Sourcing (SRC) capability aims to streamline the strategic planning and development
of the IT supply base to optimize the contribution of the supply base to the organization’s
strategic objectives. Establish a common approach to selecting IT suppliers for their operational contribution
and potential strategic impact, instead of awarding contracts only or mainly on the basis
of lowest bid price.
Assess the value and relevance of current and potential sourcing opportunities and
relationships according to long-term goals and overall business and supply management
objectives.
Achieve both cost reduction and improvement in IT supplier performance.
Ensure continuity of supply if a supplier’s operations are unexpectedly disrupted or when
switching suppliers.
Leverage good practice and innovations from the supply base to support business
innovation.
Increase organizational effectiveness by simplifying, automating, and integrating sourcing
processes across the organization.
column6: Develop IT’s own contract negotiation position in advance (for example, by identifying negotiable and non-negotiable items, and considering incentives). Understand the IT service provider’s success criteria to create win-win situations. E.g.1: SPEPRO is providing operational framework contract for interinstitutional and EP-specific network equipment and services procurement e.g.2. EVOTE service is providing operational contract and supplier management for EP-specific electronic vote services. e.g.3.SOFTCLOUD is ensuring operational framework and Enterprise Licence Agreement for interinstitutional and EP-specific contracts for software licences, Cloud subscriptions and related services, as well as management of suppliers thereof; e.g.4. EQUILOG capability refers to ICT equipment & tele-communication services (e.g. individual equipment, servers, specialized equipment, telephony, SIM cards, mobile subscriptions, fiber optic, ADSL line, etc.)

column4:
The Supplier Management (SUM) capability aims to manage interactions between the IT
function and its suppliers. After the IT sourcing strategy has been defined and the suppliers have been selected, the
relationships with the suppliers have to be managed. Failure to manage these relationships
can result in adversarial interactions with the suppliers. Managing them effectively can result
in collaborative partnerships that reduce costs and risks, and create opportunities for joint
innovation and value creation [1].
By developing a Supplier Management (SUM) capability, an organization can have
an effective approach to supplier management in which individual behaviours and
organizational practices are fair, open, and honest, so that the business and operational
interests of both the organization and its suppliers mutually respect each other. Ultimately,
the organization can build trust and create an environment in which the organization and its
suppliers collaborate to their mutual benefit.
column6: Manage risks associated with the continuity of supply from external sources e.g.1. SOFTCLOUD identifies the compliance and budgetary risks steaming from the evoluation of software and cloud services´ suppliers as well as identifying risks from software and cloud services´lifecycle issues (e.g. input to Technology Clock team); SOFTCLOUD is also offering optimisation and governance of software & cloud licenses and assets, as well as enforcing SLAs with contractors (e.g. resellers and suppliers)  e.g.2. EQUILOG´s capability refers to ICT equipment & tele-communication services (e.g. individual equipment, servers, specialized equipment, telephony, SIM cards, mobile subscriptions, fiber optic, ADSL line, etc.)

It includes the following: 1. Supplier Relationships
Develop supplier engagement appropriate to supplier classification and foster long-term strategic relationships with preferred suppliers.
2. Supplier Communication and Governance
Ensure appropriate communication channels are in place to address problems as they arise. Plan, manage, and execute the exchange of information with appropriate governance and performance oversight structures.
3. Supplier Qualification
Establish suppliers on procurement systems and qualified supplier lists.                             4. Manage supplier qualification for the full range of IT products and services, including supplier phase-out in a controlled manner.
5. Service Transition
Support the introduction of new IT services or the migration of existing IT services between the IT supplier and the organization, considering all pertinent aspects (for example, staffing the project team, preparing technical interfaces, migrating data, security protocols, and defining access rights). e.g. 1. SOFTCLOUD provides decision making support to AOs and central teams (e.g. SSP team) on the possible procurement channels available for suppliers or for a specific business need in the domain of software/cloud products and services.; SOFTCLOUD provides operational contract management for inter-institutional software and cloud procurement contracts, facilitating communication between EP stakeholders and resellers, brokers (e.g. EC), suppliers, inter-institutional partners; e.g.2. EQUILOG´s capability refers to ICT equipment & tele-communication services (e.g. individual equipment, servers, specialized equipment, telephony, SIM cards, mobile subscriptions, fiber optic, ADSL line, etc.)

Regular analysis and reporting on different aspects concerning staff, posts and other related topics, in order to support continuous improvement, data-driven adjustments and evidence-based decision-making.

column2: The Human Capital capability covers the full HR lifecycle for EP staff: recruitment, trainings, career, pay & pensions and working conditions.
column4: The People Asset Management capability is the ability to meet the organization's requirements for an effective workforce.
column6:
Define a staff sourcing strategy aligned both with DG’s purpose, values and strategic objectives, and with the specific needs of different units or services. Performing gap analysis to identify critical skill gaps, anticipate future staff needs, and develop targeted recruitment and development strategies to bridge those gaps, so that the services continuity and their best possible operation to be ensured.  
 
HR part of sourcing strategy is well-developed, resting on the assumption of the insufficiency of the current mainstream talent databases (EPSO, CAST P), thus actively working with all internal actors (DG PERS, SG HR team) in order to develop alternative talent sources via calls for expressions of interest, competitions and selection procedures organised by Parliament etc. Intensified outreach activities, both regular and ad hoc, further facilitate this objective.
 
Free texts of published vacancies are regularly updated and reviewed to help attract the right candidates and enhance the institution's appeal as an employer. ITEC job profiles are also regularly reviewed, so that they accurately reflect the current duties, responsibilities, and qualifications required for each role, and keeping them aligned with the organisation's strategic objectives and IT industry tendencies.  
 
Regular dialogue on HR matters is carried on all hierarchical levels. HR team handles individual staff contacts and queries, while general communication on HR matters is done in close conjunction with CUSTEX Unit’s communication team. Dialogue and consultation with staff on HR matters should be further developed.

Recruitment capabilities include researching multiple sources (EPSO, CAST P, GESTMAX including ITEC cyber call for expression of interest database and spontaneous candidacies) and implementing specific selection programmes (calls for expression of interest, internalisation and internal programmes such as SRDP), as well as preparing vacancies publication, organising, conducting interviews and evaluating candidates, and collaborating with further stakeholders during the recruitment process (COPAR and COSCON joint committees,  DG PERS,  SG HR team). column4: The Data Analytics capability is the ability to support data-driven decision-making both at the political and at the administrative level in the EP. It will facilitate innovation by fostering the usage of data science methodologies. Ultimately, this will lead to increased data literacy and a thriving data culture in view of paving the way towards the productive implementation of innovative data-driven technologies such as Artificial Intelligence (AI), Machine Learning and Big Data analytics at the European Parliament.
column6:
The Data Analytics Learning & Knowledge Sharing capability is the ability to develop skills and expertise in the field of data science and data analytics.   

The Data Analytics learning and knowledge base at the EP is accessible to IT and business colleagues across the EP via the EP Data Science Lab portal which provides: 

 * News, learning resources and practical guidance on data science and data analytics, in a format that is accessible to anyone interested in making the most of data-driven decision-making in their professional context.

 * Tools and methodological guidance of interest to colleagues with a more advanced level of technical knowledge in the field of data analytics, who would like to further develop their analytics capabilities and to keep themselves abreast of new applications and technological developments.

 * Best practices and case studies in applying data science and AI solutions in professional contexts, aimed at inspiring and empowering colleagues from business-oriented profiles to succeed when running data-driven projects.

column4: The Data Management capability defines the scope of capabilities required to establish, enable and sustain a mature data management discipline. It addresses the strategies, organizational structures, technology and operational best practices needed to successfully drive data management. It addresses the principles of data management based on an understanding of business value combined with the reality of operational implementation.
column5: Corporate-level Data Production, Integration and Utilisation
column6:
This capability will enable users (data owners) to conform their data to the EP Data Policy and will end-enable users to access and reuse FAIR data. It includes:
●         Data unification service
The Data Unification service provides data owner a way to make their data available in a standard, interoperable form.
●         Data acquisition service
The Data Acquisition service enables manual adding/updating of data directly to the EP data platform (in addition to the Data Unification service).
●         Data catalogue service
The Data Catalogue service provides for collecting, documenting and accessing information about data assets.
●         Data alignment capability
The Data Alignment capability provides the capability that independently managed internal and external datasets are semantically integrated and experienced as a single dataset.
●         Data access service
Data Access service provides standard-based way for people and applications to access the corporate EP data.
●         Data update service
The Data Update service ensures that the data (on the Platform) is up-to-date and previous states are accessible (versioning). The duration of availability need to be defined by the data owner and would last for maximum XXX years.
●         Data analytics service
The Data Analytics service has user interface providing an example of data utilisation of the Data Platform.

Capability to convert already available EP public data into an open format (technical, legal, practical and social openness) and then make this data available to anyone for consumption via ‘Data.europa.eu – The official portal for European data’.
 
 In this context open data has been defined as ‘any data that is made available with an open licence’ or, following the definition of the Open Knowledge Foundation, ‘data that can be freely used, re-used and distributed by anyone’.

column4:
The Innovation Management (IM) capability helps exploit IT in new and pioneering ways to satisfy business objectives. Increase innovation throughput from the IT function by fostering a pioneering culture informed by approaches, methods, and tools for innovative thinking and problem solving. Incentivize collaboration with other business units to identify novel uses of IT to support business operations, products, and services.
Improve accountability for innovation management by measuring business value and other meaningful metrics.
Promote informed risk taking – for example, by:
Harvesting key learnings from unsuccessful innovation initiatives using non-accusatory approaches.
Supporting innovative performance of teams and individual employees.
Providing more control over how employees approach solving business challenges. By developing an effective Innovation Management (IM) capability, an organization can implement appropriate innovation practices that fit with its specific context, allowing the IT function to facilitate technology-driven business innovation.
column6:
The Innovative Ideas Collection capability is the ability for EP staff to submit ideas on the EP Innovate platform. All the ideas are collected and then they are evaluated by the designated Space Managers, in line with simple criteria specific to each space, in order to identify the ideas with the highest potential.
Technological innovation ideas, for example, are scored according to criteria related to novelty and alignment with corporate and unit objectives.
The highest scoring innovative ideas are explored further to better understand the needs and to analyse - and where appropriate  to test through a proof of concept -  the feasibility of turning them into reality. The goal is to make the broader innovation process at the EP as easy and as informed as possible.

column4:
The IT Solution Delivery capability is the ability to design, develop, validate, and
deploy IT solutions that effectively address the organization’s business requirements and
opportunities. The IT Solution Delivery capability covers:
Managing requirements (functional and non-functional) and their traceability throughout
the IT solution’s delivery life cycle.
Developing IT solutions based on the output from requirements analysis and the
solution’s architecture.
Selecting appropriate methods and IT solutions delivery life cycle models (for example,
waterfall, incremental, agile).
Reviewing and testing IT solutions throughout the development process.
Managing changes and releases that occur during the IT solution’s delivery life cycle.

waterfall, incremental, agile).

Managing changes and releases that occur during the IT solution’s delivery life cycle.

column2: The Information Technology capability is the ability to deliver information and communication technology (ICT) services and solutions to the Parliament in line with current and future business needs whilst leveraging, innovation opportunities, to continually improve the ICT offering to Members and staff.
column4:
The Research, Development and Engineering (RDE) capability is the ability to investigate, acquire, develop, and evaluate technologies, solutions, and usage models that are new to the organization and might offer value. It covers:
Ensuring that research into new technologies is managed appropriately, so that risk to the organization is minimized, while opportunities are maximized.
Linking research into new technology to potential usage models that can benefit business units.
Coordinating a research pipeline of promising new technology projects, through a series of phased investment decisions, as understanding of feasibility and relevance is enhanced.
Managing the research portfolio to better align with business goals.
Instilling an organizational culture that promotes research and innovation.
Measuring the value contributed by technology research activities.
column6:
The Innovative Solution Prototyping capability is the ability to engage in an experimental process in which ideas are turned into tangible solutions of varying degrees of fidelity.
 
In the context of the Agile IT Programme (AITP) these innovative projects which will deliver prototypes/proofs of concept will not be deployed into production. They will be part of the AITP but will be subject to a lighter process for security and data protection. This is because these projects are in the realm of experimentation and their main goal is to gain insight into how leading-edge technologies could potentially provide business value to Parliament in future. If a POC result is successful then a second project to implement the idea in production, subject to all the normal AITP procedures e.g., Cost Benefit Analysis, detailed security checks etc., would be initiated to address a specific client need.

The Service Provisioning (SRP) capability is the ability to manage IT services to satisfy business requirements. This includes ongoing activities relating to operations, maintenance, and continuous service improvement, and also transitional activities relating to the introduction of services, their deployment, and their eventual decommissioning.The main purpose of this sub-business area is to deliver high quality services to:
 * the MEPs and their assistants, 
 * the staff of the European Parliament (EP),
 * the European Data Protection Supervisor (EDPS)
 * the Authority for European political parties and European political foundations (APPF). 
These include corporate services for all the users (such as the support for mobile devices and corporate applications) as well as services for specific user populations. The Service Provisioning (SRP) capability helps to ensure that the organization’s strategy and objectives are supported by reliable and effective IT services.
column6: Plan and manage technical service capacity to meet business demands in an agile way.

Manage the IT services from service introduction, through to deployment and operation, and to eventual decommissioning. Communicate any changes to the service catalogue. Can also include  specific abilities of service transition, such as: 1. Change Management 
Ensure that all changes are communicated, evaluated, and implemented in a controlled manner so that they can respond to changing business needs, and protect the business and other services. 
2. Service Validation and Testing 
Ensure that the service design and its functionality deliver a new or changed service that meets the needs of the business. 
3. Release and Deployment Management 
Plan, schedule, and control the build and deployment of service releases to deliver the changes and/or new functionality required by the business, while also protecting the integrity of existing services. 
4.Configuration Management 
Ensure that all service component Configuration Items (CIs) are identified and that any changes to them are authorized, recorded, and managed. e.g.1: HOSTGROW serves as "entry point" for the applications teams in order to ensure a smooth execution of the Rel&Dep process by HOSTOPER. e.g.2 SPEPRO is in charge of the service for Additional Data Network Plug in a Room; e.g.3 SUPOPS services described in the Closer Catalogue e.g.4. EVOTE service is managing the end-to-end service for electronic voting and have their own ticketing system; e.g.6 SOFTCLOUD is managing IT requests relatetd to procurement of licenses, etc. equally, provide inputs to Technology Clock Team; e.g.7. Once a common understanding on purpose, vision, goals has been agreed upon,
emphasis has to be put on the specific business objectives for each DG to best serve the EP Administration's common strategies. This is the
time to come to the definition objectives and of an adequate portfolio of projects which will enable the DG to provide new products and new processes while stopping out-dated functions. The DG's strategic programmes and projects to be incorporated into the Parliamentary Project Portfolio are adequately defined, with precise scope, timing, resources, demands for

column2: The Planning capability is concerned with the creation of the organisational vision, mission and strategic objectives and the necessary  strategic and operational plans to achieve the desired strategic direction.
column4:
The Strategic Planning capability is the ability to:
 
1. Debate and agree at managerial level on the specific ideation, nature and vision of the DG as a specific contributor to the EP Administration's common general purpose, goals and strategies. "What does this common purpose, identity and intention mean for us? How does it challenge us to change?" 
 
2. Define the strategic goals of the DG and its contribution to the wider strategic goals of the EP. Furthermore, it includes the definition of the DG's programmes and project portfolio in line with the stated strategic goals.
 
3.Contribute to the setting up of the EP's multi-annual budgetary plan and its annual review and potential adjustment.
column6: Mutually align and integrate IT and business strategic plans. Determine the IT strategy's contribution to business objectives. Influence the formation of the business strategy regarding the use of technology-enabled solutions to overcome business challenges. e.g. ENAR Unit is consolidating across all DG ITEC Units the link between Unit objectives and ITE strategic orientations.

column6: Formulate the long-term scope and objectives for IT value generation (IT vision), and create high-level guidelines for deploying technology-enabled services (IT design principles). e.g. ENAR Unit respectively BAAR service have been defining the enterprise architecture guiding principles

column2: The Security capability is the ability of the European Parliament to enable the smooth running of its activities whilst ensuring the protection of people, infrastructure and information.
column4:
The Risk Management (RM) capability is the ability to identify, assess, prioritize, treat, and monitor the exposure to and the potential impact of IT-related risks that can directly affect the business. Risks include traditional IT risks and those more specific to the transformational changes brought about by new and emerging technologies; they include those mainly associated with IT security, data protection and information privacy, business operations, continuity of business and recovery from declared disasters, IT investment and project/service delivery, and IT service contracts and suppliers.
 
CISO contributes to EP Risk Register and owns the ITEC RR.
column6:
Identify subject matter experts (SMEs) for risk assessments. Run risk assessments to identify, document, evaluate exposure to, and quantify/score risks and their components. Record the results in a risk register.
 
CISO contributes for DG ITEC

column2:
The Technical Infrastructure Management (TIM) capability is the ability to manage an organization’s IT infrastructure across the complete life cycle of: 
 Transitional activities including building, deploying, and decommissioning infrastructure. 
 Operational activities including operation, maintenance, and continual improvement of 
infrastructure. 
IT infrastructure is comprised of: 
 Physical devices – for example, servers, storage, and mobile devices. 
 Virtual devices/resources – for example, virtual storage and virtual networks. 
 Infrastructure-related software – for example, middleware, operating systems, and 
firmware. 
 Communications components – for example, LAN/WAN, Wi-Fi, MPLS, and voice 
infrastructure. 
 Platform services – for example, content management and web services. 
 IT infrastructure governance – for example, asset management and configuration 
management.
column4: Groups together organization´s specific capabilities in relation to: Operating System / IaaS, Virtualization, Containers and Middleware
column6:
The Application Platform capability includes the following: 
1. Operating System / IaaS: Provide life cycle management for operating systems (OS) and platforms (that run an OS to provide a function) in the organization.

2. Containers: Manage the platforms that facilitate the organization and virtualization of software containers.e.g.1: HOSTGROW is contributing to the containerization strategy, implement the tools
 
3: Middleware: Manage the software that acts as a bridge between the operating system or database and applications. e.g.1: APC in charge of consolidating and maintaining the Business Agnostic Architecture, including middleware, databases, operating systems and hardware (high level; e,g, 2: HOSTOPER manages the middleware for apps (on-prem or in the Cloud)

The Technical Infrastructure Management (TIM) capability is the ability to manage an organization’s IT infrastructure across the complete life cycle of: 
 Transitional activities including building, deploying, and decommissioning infrastructure. 
 Operational activities including operation, maintenance, and continual improvement of 
infrastructure. 
IT infrastructure is comprised of: 
 Physical devices – for example, servers, storage, and mobile devices. 
 Virtual devices/resources – for example, virtual storage and virtual networks. 
 Infrastructure-related software – for example, middleware, operating systems, and 
firmware. 
 Communications components – for example, LAN/WAN, Wi-Fi, MPLS, and voice 
infrastructure. 
 Platform services – for example, content management and web services. 
 IT infrastructure governance – for example, asset management and configuration 
management.
column4: Groups together organization´s specific capabilities in relation to: Storage, Connectivity /Network, Data Centre Environment, Device Management , Infrastructure Related Software & Software Defined Infrastructure
column6:
Manage the lifecycle of all multifunctional copiers, as well as the development of associated additional services, for the benefit of customers.
Provide a DIY printing or any printer is your printer.
Delivery of the physical hardware (the machine itself).

column6:
Manage the deployment and utilization of IT infrastructure assets, including software
licenses, networks, physical devices, and virtual devices. Redeploy, retire, and acquire assets as needed. e.g.1. SOFTCLOUD offers optimization and governance of software and cloud licenses and assets, including management of intangible financial assets in FMS; e.g.2 LOGOPS is responsible for keeping track of the utilisation of ICT equipment logistics throughout their complete lifecycle at the EP as well as third party maintenance (including the generation of bar codes for all new assets, sticking them on the asset, as well as annual tracking exercise); end of life of the asset (ad-hoc or systematically set-up, going back to stocks and followed by write-off - revalorization or recycling); equipment like mouse/keyboards are qualified consumables, therefore not tracked in the lifecycle after end-user receipt.

column6: Capacity planning/ elasticity refers to managing the level of IT infrastructure resources that are needed to support the organization's business production requirements, to cover both peaks and troughs in demand, while ensuring that key performance indicators (KPIs), service levels, and budgets are in compliance with the organization's goals and objectives.Security refers to ensuring the security and integrity of the IT infrastructure via firewall management, intrusion detection, vulnerability scanning and detection, anti-viral services, staff vetting, audit reporting, and so on. E.g.1: Le service CAPAC est responsable pour superviser, gérer et opérer 24h/24 le réseau IP (réseau de base inter-sites, réseau local sur les 3 sites, réseau WIFI, liens vers bureaux de liaison, infrastructure TOIP, infrastructure Visioconférence, infrastructure encodeurs VOD, connectivité extérieure (Internet Service Provider, autres institutions, autres administrations).

Manage identities, and their authentication, authorization, roles, and privileges so that data and IT assets (only those integrated with IAM) are protected from inappropriate and unauthorized access. Managing identity content implies dealing with usernames, passwords, and other personal information associated with individuals within the EP.
By Application:
- Access control
- Content management
By Deployment Type:
- On-Premises
- Cloud

Plan, test, and put in place what needs to be done to continue to deliver IT infrastructure services for defined scenarios. Plan, test, and execute disaster management scenarios, so that the organization can recover as quickly as possible. Respond to attacks in such a way that forensic data is secured, and that any identified vulnerabilities are made secure. e.g.1: le service CAPAC est responsable pour assurer des services de continuité opérationnelle: résilience, capacité d'effectuer un Disaster Recovery, sécurité et intégrité des infrastructures. e.g.2. EVOTE service is ensuring the availability of the electronic voting system in the hemicycles and committee rooms fas well as the availability of the SMIAP screens in the hemicycles for Plenary sessions and special events.

column6: Design and plan the introduction of changes to the IT infrastructure that are required to maintain or improve the existing IT infrastructure to support business services and solutions. e.g 1:  APC is in charge of he design for middleware, databases, operating systems and hardware (high level); e.g.2: for the digital workplace services, DWP Service is in charge of designing, architecting and engineering; LSAs are validating/check the technical solution; or email architecture and mobile device management; Cloud unit is in charge of the email architecture and telephony &video conferencing while Netserv is in charge of the infrastructure related to webstreaming, IPTV; e.g.3.  Netserv is responsible for designing the network infrastructure integration aspects while the Ops is operating it; design implies 3 levels of detail: Network architecture, High level design (define the concrete solution) & lower level design (explain the configuration to deploy per component); e.g.4 IAM Service is responsible for the Identity & Access Management service, that provides a platform and advices for authentication and authorization for any applications on-prem and in Cloud. e.g.5 EVOTE Service is responsible for maintaining and ensuring the technological evolution of the complete electronic voting specific infrastructure: hardware, software, telecommunications, firmware upgrading), e.g.6 LOGOPS is collaborating with other ESIO Units for engineering and architecture of the complete telecommunications ecosystem.

column6: Manage major technology infrastructure changes, transition and support the introduction of new components or modifications and retirements; for example, the transition to cloud, major hardware refreshes, firmware upgrading, introduction of a bring-your-own-device policy, capacity changes (e.g. netserv is in charge of network capacity) and so on. e.g. 1. APC is in charge to support in implementation of changes for middleware, databases, operating systems and hardware (high level); e.g.2: for the digital workplace services, DWP Service is in charge of designing, architecting and engineering; LSAs are validating/check the technical solution; or email architecture and mobile device management; Cloud unit is in charge of the telephony &video conferencing while Netserv is in charge of the infrastructure related to webstreaming, IPTV; e.g.3 IAM Service is responsible for The Identity & Access Management service, that provides a platform and advices for authentication and authorization for any applications on-prem and in Cloud. e.g.4: SPEPRO is responsible with cabling; e.g.5 SUPOPS est responsable avec l´intégrer des infrastructures supportant des nouveaux services/systèmes dans la gestion opérationnelle quotidienne e.g.6 EVOTE service is ensuring the interoperability with other services and applications (DG PRES, DG ITEC, DG LINC, DG COMM, DG IPOL & DG EXPO), e.g.7 LOGOPS service is ensuring cabling operations covering the end-point provisioning of wired connectivity to higher-level services, such as Telephony, Videoconferencing, IP-Network extensions, and TV distribution services.

APC is in charge of monitoring product evolutions, participate in the recommendation assessment for future evolutions of products in their scope; e.g.2: Cloud Unit is supporting the work carried out by the Governance bodies (e.g. preparing agenda, subject matter on the cloud topics, etc.); new topics like data protection aspects, low coding platforms, cloud providers selection and evolution, are strategic themes and Cloud Gov. Board is the right place of discussion; e.g. 3 SOFTCLOUD is supporting internal customers to achieve savings on software and Cloud costs enabling immediate savings and delivering increased long-term efficiencies and cost reductions; from the video-conferecing perspective, SOFTCLOUD delivers operational improvements for hybrid conferencing enabling increased efficiency.
